hero.title: The Bankers of George's Street
hero.subtitle: How a dispossessed Norman family armed a ship, built a bank, survived a crisis, and grew pineapples in Limerick

media.alt: Castle Roche, County Louth — a 13th-century Norman castle on its rocky outcrop, now a national monument
media.caption: Castle Roche, County Louth — the Normans who carried the name arrived in 1169

media.alt: The Dublin Journal, 1758, reporting on Plantation News and West Indies trade — the world John Lysaght Roche dispatched his armed merchant ship into
media.caption: The Dublin Journal, 1758 — 'Plantation News' columns chronicled the West Indies trade that made the Roche fortune

media.alt: The Sun, London, 1802 — a Limerick citizens' meeting resolving that subscriptions be deposited at 'the Banks of Thomas and William Roche'
media.caption: 1802 — the Bank of Thomas and William Roche named as a deposit address at a Limerick citizens' meeting

media.alt: A terraced garden with climbing plants — evoking the Hanging Gardens of Roche, which rose seventy feet behind the George's Street bank
media.caption: William Roche built terraced gardens rising seventy feet — pineapples, peaches, and oranges above the vaulted stone stores

media.alt: George's Street, Limerick — a Lawrence photograph showing the Georgian commercial street where the Roche bank and its competitors once stood
media.caption: George's Street, Limerick — in 1820 every private bank on this street failed, except one

media.alt: Daniel O'Connell, 'The Champion of Liberty' — lithograph by A. M. Hoffy, 1847. Close friend and kinsman of William Roche M.P.
media.caption: O'Connell declared: 'William Roche was the only man in Ireland from whom he would not demand a pledge'

media.alt: Leinster Express obituary of Thomas Roche, 22 December 1855 — 'surviving partner of the late Bank of Thomas and William Roche'
media.caption: December 1855 — 'the only one, probably, in the South of Ireland, that passed through the disastrous commercial crash with perfect honour and integrity'
